header: 3  The Update Is the Group’s Standard Deviation

images.caption: Figure 3:The group-standard-deviation identity.Left:the per-prompt gradient atG=8G=8, zero when all samples agree and largest at an even split, landing onk​(G−k)/G\sqrt{k(G-k)}/G; Monte-Carlo markers at three baselinesb∈{0.2,0.5,0.8}b\in\{0.2,0.5,0.8\}.Right:the realized fraction𝔼​[g]/p​(1−p)\mathbb{E}[g]/\sqrt{p(1-p)}against the asymptotic law1−1/(8​G​p​(1−p))1-1/(8Gp(1-p))of Corollary1.

header: 4  How Many Samples a Prompt Needs

images.caption: Figure 4:The group-size law.Left:the gradient fidelityφ​(G,p)=𝔼​[g]/p​(1−p)\varphi(G,p)=\mathbb{E}[g]/\sqrt{p(1-p)}against group size for four difficulties, with the closed form1−1/(8​G​p​(1−p))1-1/(8Gp(1-p))dashed.Right:the group size required for95%95\%and99%99\%fidelity against difficulty, exact (solid) and the lawG⋆G^{\star}(dashed).

header: 5  Silent Groups, and What DAPO Discards

images.caption: Figure 5:The silent-group ratepG+(1−p)Gp^{G}+(1-p)^{G}of (10). Groups are most often silent near the easy and hard extremes, where all sampled answers tend to share the same reward. LargerGGreduces the silent mass in the interior but cannot remove the endpointsp=0p=0andp=1p=1.

Figure 6:DAPO’s discarded-prompt fraction and the closed form.Left:DAPO’s logged accuracy-11fraction (avg@32, Fig. 3b[8]), the closed-form𝔼p​[p32]\mathbb{E}_{p}[p^{32}]fit with bootstrap1010–90%90\%band, and a generic saturating baseline.Right:the closed-form all-correct mass𝔼p​[p32]\mathbb{E}_{p}[p^{32}]versus policy competence on Big-Math, with the frozen anchor and DAPO’s plateau.

Figure 7:The large-group limit of the identity.Left:raw success ratepp(Dr. GRPO) against the arcsine transform2π​arcsin⁡p\frac{2}{\pi}\arcsin\sqrt{p}(GRPO).Right:expected per-prompt gradient: closed-form curves with Monte-Carlo markers over groups of sizeG=64G=64on a Bernoulli-logit prompt.

Figure 8:The difficulty weightw​(p)=∂p2​arcsin⁡p=1/p​(1−p)w(p)=\partial_{p}\,2\arcsin\sqrt{p}=1/\sqrt{p(1-p)}of (11) (log scale), against the flat weightw≡1w\equiv 1of Dr. GRPO. GRPO assigns extra marginal weight to the easiest and hardest prompts, while Dr. GRPO weights each unit of raw success-rate improvement equally.

header: 7  The Lens on Real Difficulty Data

images.caption: Figure 9:Real difficulty distribution and the standardization reweighting (Big-Math,N=215,608N=215{,}608).Left:histogram of empirical solve ratesp^\hat{p}(Llama-3.1-8B, 64 rollouts).Right:share of per-prompt gradient budget by difficulty under Dr. GRPO (∝p​(1−p)\propto p(1-p)) and GRPO (∝p​(1−p)\propto\sqrt{p(1-p)}).

images.caption: Figure 10:The identity’s predictions in a controlled GRPO run (M=6,000M=6{,}000Bernoulli-logit prompts, Big-Math initial difficulty,G=8G=8).(a)measured silent-group fraction against the closed form over training.(b)realized gradient mass by difficulty, measured against the finite-GGclosed form.(c)mean solve rate of the initially-hardest quartile under GRPO, Dr. GRPO, and DAPO.
